title: Force on a dam: pressure grows with depth, force is the area
scenario: A vertical wall $w = 5$ m wide holds back fresh water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) to a depth of $H = 4$ m. The water pressure grows with depth as $P = \rho g h$, so the wall is pushed hardest at the bottom and not at all at the surface. What is the total force on the wall? It is the *area* under the pressure curve — drag the cursor to raise the water level and watch the force grow as the square of the depth.

assumptions.claim: Incompressible fluid of constant density $\rho$; gauge pressure (atmospheric pushes equally on both faces of the wall and cancels).
$g = 10$ m/s² as a magnitude, with depth $h$ measured positive *downward* (the house $g = -10$ convention is for up-positive kinematics; here pressure simply grows with depth).
A flat vertical wall, so each horizontal strip has the same width $w$; the force is horizontal and normal to the wall.

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you
steps.latex: F = \bar P\,A \qquad \bar P = \tfrac12\rho g H \quad(\text{pressure at the centroid})
steps.prose: The algebra course says the force is the *average* pressure times the wall area. Because the pressure runs from $0$ at the surface to $\rho g H$ at the bottom, it takes the average as the midpoint value $\tfrac12\rho g H$. That gives the right number — but only because the pressure rises *linearly*, and the algebra cannot say why the average is the midpoint rather than something else.

wall_force.label: Force on the wall $F = \tfrac12\rho g w H^2$ (the shaded area)

bottom_pressure.label: Pressure at the bottom $P = \rho g H$

uniform_rectangle.label: If the pressure were the bottom value everywhere: $F = \rho g H\cdot A$ (the rectangle — twice the force)

steps.label: The force is the area under the pressure–depth curve
steps.prose: Each horizontal strip of the wall at depth $h$ (width $w$, height $dh$) feels a force $dF = P(h)\,w\,dh$. The total force is the integral over the depth — the area under the strip-force curve. For a uniform pressure this area is a rectangle ($P_0 A$); when the pressure grows with depth it is the area under a rising line.

steps.label: Use the hydrostatic law $P(h) = \rho g h$
steps.latex: F = \int_0^H \rho g w\,h\,dh = \tfrac12\rho g w H^2 = \underbrace{\tfrac12\rho g H}_{\bar P}\,\underbrace{wH}_{A}
steps.prose: With $P = \rho g h$ the integrand is a straight line through the origin, so the area is a triangle: $\tfrac12\,(\text{base})\,(\text{height}) = \tfrac12\,H\,(\rho g w H)$. That is $\tfrac12\rho g w H^2$ — and factoring it as $(\tfrac12\rho g H)(wH)$ shows the algebra's $\bar P A$ is exactly this area, with the centroid pressure emerging as the average. Its slope at any depth is the strip force.

steps.label: Why the depth, not the lake, sets the force
steps.prose: If the pressure did not grow with depth — a gas at uniform $P_0$ pressing on the wall — the integrand would be constant and the integral would collapse to the rectangle $P_0 A$. The real force is only *half* the bottom-pressure rectangle $\rho g H\cdot A$ because the pressure is small near the surface. And it depends only on the depth and the wall's width, not on how much water is behind it.

proof.heading: The force on the wall is the area under the pressure-times-width curve — and the average-pressure formula falls out.
proof.detail: check $\tfrac{dF}{dh} = \rho g w h$; check $F = \int_0^h \rho g w\,h'\,dh'$; recover $F = \bar P A$; collapse the uniform-pressure case to $P_0 A$

checks.claim: The force's slope is the strip force: $F'(h) = \rho g w h$ — the area's growth rate is the curve's height.
The accumulated force is the area: $F(h) = \int_0^h \rho g w\,h'\,dh'$.
The memorized $F = \bar P A$ (average pressure $\bar P = \rho g h/2$ at the centroid, area $A = wh$) is exactly the area.
If the pressure did not grow with depth ($P_0$ uniform), the integral collapses to $F = P_0 A$ — a rectangle (the quadrature).

misconception.claim: The force on a dam depends on how much water is behind it — a wall holding back a huge lake feels far more force than one holding back a narrow tank of the same depth.
misconception.refuted_by: It does not. The pressure at a given depth is $\rho g h$ regardless of how much water sits at that level — a teaspoon or an ocean. The force on the wall is the area under that pressure profile, $\tfrac12\rho g w H^2$, set entirely by the *depth* and the wall's *width*. This is the hydrostatic paradox: raise the water a little (drag the cursor) and the force jumps because it grows as $H^2$, but widen the lake behind the wall and nothing changes.
